sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 16, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 15 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE again teleported the player back to cols 14-18 (the start). So SPACE always teleports to the start, regardless of where the player is. This is confirmed.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics. I've been assuming the player needs to physically move through the maze, but maybe that's not the case. Let me look at the image more carefully.
Looking at the current image:
- Player is at top-left (cols 14-18)
- There's a red path on the right side
- There's a target (blue square) at the bottom-right
- The maze has white corridors
Wait, I just noticed something. The red path in the current image shows a horizontal segment at the bottom (rows ~38-40) going from the right side to the left. This is different from the initial state where the red path was just a small L-shape.
Let me check the red path's current state. Earlier, when the player first reached the red path, it unfolded from 82 pixels to 66 pixels. But now, after resetting with SPACE, the red path is back to 82 pixels.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the animation from when the player first reached the red path. The animation showed the red path changing shape. The boundary changed from:
- Before: [[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18]]
- After: [[13, 40], [40, 40], [40, 25], [41, 24], [42, 24], [42, 20], [41, 21], [40, 20], [39, 21], [38, 20], [38, 24], [39, 24], [40, 25], [40, 39], [39, 40]]
The red path changed from an L-shape to a more complex shape. The pixel count changed from 82 to 66.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the game is about the player "collecting" or "activating" the red path segments. When the player reaches the red path, it changes shape. The goal might be to change the red path into a specific shape that matches the target.
Or maybe the game is about the player following the red path, and the red path "moves" or "extends" as the player progresses.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the full maze structure and see if there's a path from the player's starting position to the target.
Actually, let me step back and think about what I know:
1. The player can move LEFT/RIGHT along the top corridor.
2. SPACE resets the player to the start.
3. DOWN doesn't work (the player can't move down).
4. The red path changes shape when the player reaches it.
5. The target is at the bottom-right.
Wait, maybe I've been misunderstanding the game. Let me look at the image again. The player is a blue square with a black dot. The target is also a blue square with a black dot. They look similar! Maybe the game is about the player "becoming" the target, or the target "becoming" the player.
Actually, looking more carefully, the target at the bottom-right is a blue square with a black dot inside, similar to the player. But the player is at the top-left. Maybe the game is about moving the player to the target's position.
But the player can only move LEFT/RIGHT, not DOWN. So how can the player reach the target at the bottom-right?
Wait, maybe the game uses a different mechanic. Let me look at the red path again. The red path seems to be a "bridge" or "connector" between the player's position and the target. When the player reaches the red path, it changes shape. Maybe the red path is meant to "connect" the player to the target.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the player to the far right (where the red path starts) and see what happens. Maybe the red path extends further when the player reaches the end of it.
Actually, I already did this earlier. When the player reached the red path (cols 38-42), the red path changed shape. But the player couldn't move DOWN.
Let me try pressing SPACE when the player is at the far right (adjacent to the red path). Maybe this triggers a different behavior.
Wait, I already tried this. SPACE always teleports to the start.
Hmm, let me think about this more carefully. The player can move LEFT/RIGHT. SPACE resets to start. The red path changes when the player reaches it. The target is at the bottom-right.
Maybe the game is about the player "painting" the red path by moving along it. The red path is a trail that the player needs to follow. When the player reaches the end of the red path, it extends further, revealing more of the path.
But the player can only move LEFT/RIGHT, not DOWN. So how can the player follow the red path which goes DOWN?
Wait, maybe the player doesn't need to physically follow the red path. Maybe the player needs to "activate" the red path by reaching certain positions, and the red path then "moves" or "extends" on its own.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the red path's boundary more carefully and see if there's a pattern.
The red path boundary (current state):
[[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18]]
This shows:
- Top: rows 9-12, cols 39-41 (vertical segment)
- Middle: row 40, col 40 (vertical segment continues)
- Bottom: rows 40-42, cols 18-19 (horizontal segment going left)
The red path is an L-shape: vertical on the right, horizontal at the bottom going left.
When the player reaches the red path (cols 38-42), the red path changes to:
[[13, 40], [40, 40], [40, 25], [41, 24], [42, 24], [42, 20], [41, 21], [40, 20], [39, 21], [38, 20], [38, 24], [39, 24], [40, 25], [40, 39], [39, 40]]
This shows a more complex shape with multiple segments.
I'm getting confused. Let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the game from a high level and see if there's a pattern I'm missing.
The maze has:
- A top corridor where the player moves
- A red path on the right side
- White spaces below the top corridor
- A target at the bottom-right
Maybe the game is about the player "unlocking" different parts of the maze by reaching certain positions. The red path might be a key or a trigger.
Let me try moving the player to different positions and pressing SPACE to see if it triggers different behaviors. I already know SPACE teleports to the start, but maybe there's a condition where SPACE does something different.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the game description. The valid actions are UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE. I've tried LEFT, RIGHT, DOWN, and SPACE. I haven't tried UP yet!
Let me try UP from the current position. Maybe the player can move UP to a different corridor.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 24:
        print(f"Player: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try UP - haven't tried this yet!
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
